examples.title: Basic Python Logging Setup
examples.description: Demonstrates basic logging configuration with console and file handlers
examples.code:
import logging

# Configure logging
logging.basicConfig(
 level=logging.DEBUG,
 format='%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s',
 handlers=[
 logging.FileHandler('app.log'),
 logging.StreamHandler()
 ]
)

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# Example usage
logger.debug('Debug message')
logger.info('Info message')
logger.warning('Warning message')
logger.error('Error message')
logger.critical('Critical message')
examples.explanation: Shows basic logging setup with multiple handlers (file and console) and formatted output with timestamps

examples.title: Structured Logging with JSON
examples.description: Implements structured logging using JSON format for machine-parseable logs
examples.code:
import logging
import json
from datetime import datetime

class JSONFormatter(logging.Formatter):
 def format(self, record):
 log_data = {
 'timestamp': datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
 'level': record.levelname,
 'logger': record.name,
 'message': record.getMessage(),
 'module': record.module,
 'function': record.funcName,
 'line': record.lineno
 }
 if record.exc_info:
 log_data['exception'] = self.formatException(record.exc_info)
 return json.dumps(log_data)

# Setup structured logging
handler = logging.FileHandler('structured.log')
handler.setFormatter(JSONFormatter())
logger = logging.getLogger('structured')
logger.addHandler(handler)
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

# Usage
logger.info('User logged in', extra={'user_id': '12345'})
examples.explanation: JSON formatting enables easier log parsing, searching, and integration with log analysis tools

examples.title: Log4j Configuration Example
examples.description: XML configuration for Log4j with multiple appenders and level settings
examples.code:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Configuration status="WARN">
 <Appenders>
 <Console name="Console" target="SYSTEM_OUT">
 <PatternLayout pattern="%d{HH:mm:ss.SSS} [%t] %-5level %logger{36} - %msg%n"/>
 </Console>
 <RollingFile name="RollingFile" fileName="logs/app.log" filePattern="logs/app-%d{yyyy-MM-dd}-%i.log.gz">
 <JsonLayout complete="false" compact="true">
 <KeyValuePair key="timestamp" value="$${date:yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSSZ}"/>
 <KeyValuePair key="level" value="$${level}"/>
 <KeyValuePair key="logger" value="$${logger}"/>
 <KeyValuePair key="message" value="$${message}"/>
 </JsonLayout>
 <Policies>
 <TimeBasedTriggeringPolicy/>
 <SizeBasedTriggeringPolicy size="10 MB"/>
 </Policies>
 </RollingFile>
 </Appenders>
 <Loggers>
 <Root level="info">
 <AppenderRef ref="Console"/>
 <AppenderRef ref="RollingFile"/>
 </Root>
 </Loggers>
</Configuration>
examples.explanation: Shows Log4j2 configuration with console output, rolling file appender with size and time-based policies

examples.title: Custom Exception Logger with Context
examples.description: Demonstrates logging exceptions with additional context and correlation IDs
examples.explanation: Demonstrates capturing exceptions with context, unique error IDs for tracking, and using decorators for consistent error logging

examples.title: Asynchronous Logging with Queue
examples.description: Implements non-blocking asynchronous logging using Python queue
examples.code:
import logging
import threading
import queue
from datetime import datetime

class AsyncLogger:
 def __init__(self, name, log_file, level=logging.INFO):
 self.logger = logging.getLogger(name)
 self.logger.setLevel(level)
 handler = logging.FileHandler(log_file)
 handler.setFormatter(logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s'))
 self.logger.addHandler(handler)
 
 self.log_queue = queue.Queue()
 self.running = True
 self.worker_thread = threading.Thread(target=self._process_logs)
 self.worker_thread.daemon = True
 self.worker_thread.start()
 
 def _process_logs(self):
 while self.running:
 try:
 level, message = self.log_queue.get(timeout=1)
 if level == 'STOP':
 break
 getattr(self.logger, level.lower())(message)
 except queue.Empty:
 continue
 
 def log(self, level, message):
 self.log_queue.put((level, message))
 
 def stop(self):
 self.running = False
 self.log_queue.put(('STOP', ''))
 self.worker_thread.join()

# Usage
async_logger = AsyncLogger('async', 'async.log')
async_logger.log('INFO', 'Application started')
async_logger.log('ERROR', 'Connection failed')
async_logger.stop()
examples.explanation: Demonstrates asynchronous logging using a background thread and queue, preventing logging from blocking the main application
